issue_title: DidlObject.to_dict() does not return resources as a dict
issue_body:
This is the next phase of Issue #318  DidlObject.to_dict() does not return the resources as a dict.  The resources key of the returned dictionary returns a list of DidlResource objects.

To implement, there will be changes in the to_dict, and from_dict methods.  This will be a change that "could" break someone code since it is changing the returned dictionary.  It will require documentation changes as well.   And, of course, tests.

The idea was forwarded in issue #318 and generally agreed upon, but is awaiting feed back from interested parties.

issue_title: Support for self-signed certificates
issue_body:
Hi, I could not find a way for deis to support self-signed certificates in a safe way. Switching the flag "verify ssl" to false does not seem safe to me, though it may be enough for development. In case some teems would like to be using self-signed certificates for a longer time, there should be a way to tell your deis client to accept some specific certificates.

Something like requests.post(url, data=data, verify='/path/to/public_key.pem') for the python request library.

It could be as simple as: 
deis register "https://.example.com" -ca-bundle=path-to-ca-bundle
or:
deis register "https://.example.com" -ca-bundle=path-to-cert

Or any other solution that could prevent from buying a 100$ wildcard certificate and still be safe in development.

Just let me know if I missed an existing way to do that.

issue_title: Add support for asyncio (Python>=3.4)
issue_body:
It would be great if backoff would be available for use with asyncio's coroutines.

This requires:

1. Handle coroutines in `on_predicate` and `on_exception` decorators.
2. Handle case when `on_success`/`on_backoff`/`on_giveup` are coroutines.
3. Use `asyncio.sleep()` instead of `time.sleep()`.
4. Conditionally installing/importing required deps on Python < 3.4; tests; CI update.

Obviously sync and async versions of can't be trivially combined.
This can be solved in one of the following ways:

1. Check in `on_predicate`/`on_exception` is wrapped function is coroutine and switch between sync and async implementations. Notice that in general `time.sleep` can't be used with asyncio, only in separate thread due to the nature of async code. This means that both implementations - sync and async - in single program will be used very rarely.

   Also I don't see easy way of sharing code between sync/async versions. At least tests will be completely duplicated.

2. Reimplement `backoff` using async primitives in separate library. Unfortunately this leads to code duplication.

As starting point I forked `backoff` and reimplemented it with async primitives: https://github.com/rutsky/aiobackoff
It passes all tests and now I'm trying to integrate it with my project.

Please share ideas and intents of implementing asyncio support in backoff library, I would like to share efforts as much as possible.
If there are no indents of adding asyncio support to `backoff` I can publish `aiobackoff` fork.

issue_title: Switching loss type mid-training + tools repository
issue_body:
Hi,
First, thanks for creating and maintaining this great package!

Second, I have a couple of questions:

1) I noticed that on my data WARP trains much much faster than BPR, but for some cases, BPR's final accuracy is better. Also WARP sometimes overfits (validation accuracy goes down with more epochs), and with BPR I haven't observed this. 
In your opinion, does it make sense to train with WARP first and than switch to BPR for "fine tuning"? If so, what would be the best way to do this? (I see that there are various state variables for the loss and weight updates, wonder how to do it right). I've also noticed that (on my data), WARP only works well with adadelta, and BPR only with adagrad, would changing learning schedules work as well?

2) I have several implicit feedback signals (several types of interactions e.g. views and purchases), from what I've read so far from the issues, the way to correctly use them is by specifying them in sample_weight matrix. But in other places I read that it won't matter much (because the models are primarity implicit feedback ones). What is your suggestion in terms of learning from different types of interactions (all implicit, but of different significance)? Is sample_weight the right way, or do I need to create several models?

3) I have quite a lot of utility and tool code already: sampling recommendations, similarities, early stop fitting, hyperparameter tuning (via skopt), dataframes as inputs and outputs (hiding away the sparse matrices), feature matrix construction (out of an additional dataframe via sklearn-pandas), evaluation utilities.. What would be a good way to contribute this code? I read somewhere that you don't want to introduce external package dependencies, so would you prefer that as a separate repository, e.g. "lightfm-tools" or something of that sort (because there are quite a lot of pydata packages in my code)?

issue_body:
Some ABOUT files are covered by specific patents. Perhaps we can have a field dedicated to list them?

A specific case would be SZIP: https://support.hdfgroup.org/doc_resource/SZIP/Commercial_szip.html that is/was covered by patents in the US.

Perhaps possible to have optional fields such as:
```
patent: US Patent 5448642

I'm not an expert on patents, so wouldn't really know in detail how we would list patents for EU, UK and other lands. Albeit I'm sure we could likely find a way to keep this simple and intuitive.

In either case would help the tools/humans to clearly identify the existence of patents for a specific component.

Many thanks.

issue_title: Negative indexing in slice
issue_body:
Currently, we are doing Caffe2 behavior, which says negative indexes are inclusive. This allows you to pass 0:-1 to indicate that the entire range should be taken.

HOWEVER, this is inconsistent with Numpy behavior.

```
>>> import numpy
>>> x = numpy.ndarray(shape=(2,4))
>>> x[0:-1, 1:3]
array([[  5.21625023e-317,   1.16138128e-012]])
>>> x[0:, 1:3]                                                                                                  
array([[  5.21625023e-317,   1.16138128e-012],
       [  2.16449511e+233,   1.95218670e-110]])
```

This is because Python's slice syntax permits omitting the ending index to say 'go to the end'.  But in the format we've picked (IntTensor for starts and ends), this is not representable.

My suggestion for now is that we disallow negative indexing for now, pending a resolution of this problem.

issue_title: UnsupportedPythonVersion with two Pipfile [[source]] blocks
issue_body:
When installing `python-swiftclient` from a Pipfile with a single `[[source]]` block, like so:

```toml
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
python-swiftclient = {version="==3.4.0", index="pypi"}
```

`python-swiftclient` installs successfully. So far so good. But, when my Pipfile has two valid `[[source]]` blocks...

```toml
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.companyname.com"
verify_ssl = true
name = "privatepypi"

[packages]
python-swiftclient = {version="==3.4.0", index="pypi"}
```

Installation of `python-swiftclient` errors with...

```
pip.exceptions.UnsupportedPythonVersion: futures requires
Python '>=2.6, <3' but the running Python is 3.6.3
```

...despite explicitly specifying which index `python-swiftclient` should be installed from (`index="pypi"`). Why does having a second (and theoretically unused) `[[source]]` block affect installation of that package? (Of note, `privatepypi` does not contain a fork of `python-swiftclient`.)

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: macOS Sierra 10.12.6
1. Python version: 3.6.3
1. Pipenv version: 8.3.2

##### Expected result

I expected `python-swiftclient` to install from public pypi without issue when two `[[source]]` blocks are present and the correct index to use is specified for the given package.

##### Actual result

`python-swiftclient` fails to install when two `[[source]]` blocks are present, suggesting that the un-referenced `[[source]]` is being consulted or is affecting dependency resolution somehow.

##### Steps to replicate

Write this to a `Pipfile`:

```toml
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.companyname.com"
verify_ssl = true
name = "privatepypi"

[packages]
python-swiftclient = {version="==3.4.0", index="pypi"}
```

Then do `pipenv --three install` to observe the error:

```
pip.exceptions.UnsupportedPythonVersion: futures 
requires Python '>=2.6, <3' but the running Python is 3.6.3
```

Any suggestions? Thanks!

Then clone the code, install stuff, and run the test suite per the instructions in `CONTRIBUTING.rst`:

issue_title: "selective-upgrade" and update <package_name> fails with "pip install" without "-U"
issue_body:
Pipenv 11.0.2, pip 9.0.1

Running `pipenv upgrade <pkg>` results in `Requirement not upgraded as not directly required: <pkg> in ./.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages` (i.e. not a dependency, the main package).

This seems to be because a `-U` would have been needed in the pip command string, when updating (since the requested package is already installed).

issue_title: Is it possible to list which virtualenvs are managed by pipenv?
issue_body:
In my `~/.virtualenvs` folder, I have a number of environments - some are managed by `pew` and others by `pipenv`. I can't easily tell which is which (although my guess is that the ones ending in `-<random letters>` are managed by pipenv), nor do I appear to be able to identify which project directory is associated with the pipenv-managed venvs.

Basically, I'm looking for a way to do housekeeping on old or experimental projects - identify and tidy up no longer used virtual environments, determine whether it's OK to delete a project directory or if it will leave a venv behind, etc. My `~` directory is on a limited-space SSD, so I'd prefer to keep it clean.

issue_title: Is .lock a poor choice on UNIX systems?
issue_body: Have a look [here](https://github.com/golang/dep/issues/1139) and [here](https://github.com/bundler/bundler/issues/694).

issue_title: How to bootstrap setuptools from source
issue_body:
Hi, I'm a developer for the [Spack](https://spack.io/) package manager. We've had a [setuptools package](https://github.com/LLNL/spack/blob/develop/var/spack/repos/builtin/packages/py-setuptools/package.py) that has worked fine for a while now, and the vast majority of our Python packages depend on it. However, I went to update to the latest version of `setuptools` and noticed that it now depends on `six`, `packaging`, and `appdirs`. Unfortunately all 3 of these packages also depend on `setuptools`. `six` and `packaging` can fall back on `distutils.core` if necessary, but the `setup.py` for `appdirs` has a hard `setuptools` import.

It seems to me like it is no longer possible to build setuptools from source. Is this correct??

issue_title: Match args using specs/constraints rather than just equality
issue_body:
Most of the time expectations specify literal parameter values that are compared for equality against the actual parameters of invoked methods. For example:

`allow(calculator).add(2, 2).and_return(5)`

Sometimes, however, you will need to define looser constraints over parameter values to clearly express the intent of the test or to ignore parameters (or parts of parameters) that are not relevant to the behaviour being tested. For example:

```python
allow(calculator).sqrt(less_than(0)).and_raise(ValueError)
allow(log).append(equal_to(Log.ERROR), contains_string("sqrt"))
```

Loose parameter constraints can be defined by specifying matchers for each parameter. Matchers are created by factory functions, such as less_than, equal_to and contains_string in the example above, to ensure that the expectation is easy to read.

See <http://www.jmock.org/matchers.html> for motivation and details.

issue_title: `--ids` parameter only supports 3 levels
issue_body:
Azure Backup has IDs that look like this:

`/Subscriptions/00000/resourceGroups/RG/providers/Microsoft.RecoveryServices/vaults/{vault_name}/backupFabrics/{fabric_name}/protectionContainers/{container_name}/protectedItems/{item_name}/recoveryPoint/{recovery_point_guid}`

Thus they have a 5 level child resource hierarchy.  Our current `--ids` infrastructure only supports "resource", "child" and "grandchild" levels.

We should revise this feature to support an arbitrary number of things. For example, instead of:

```
        - child_namespace       Namespace for the child resoure (optional)
        - child_type            Type of the child resource
        - child_name            Name of the child resource
        - grandchild_namespace  Namespace for the grandchild resource (optional)
        - grandchild_type       Type of the grandchild resource
        - grandchild_name       Name of the grandchild resource
```

We should allow:
```
        - child1_namespace
        - child1_type            
        - child1_name          
        - child2_namespace 
        - child2_type       
        - child2_name       
```
And so forth.  This would impact the way you would need to annotate "id_part" as well as changing the structure of the dictionary "parse_resource_id" returns.

issue_title: azure.storage.StorageServiceProperties.Cors &(.CorsProperties) is missing
issue_body:
### Description:


##### Original Issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/191
### Comments (3):
#### @huguesv commented on 2014-08-18 16:54:32:
> http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/dn535601.aspx

#### @ahmetalpbalkan commented on 2014-08-18 16:57:51:
> I know the request but I couldn't create Cors or CorsProperties instances because there are no such classes, therefore I don't know what to pass to set_blob_service_properties() , could you provide an example please? 

#### @huguesv commented on 2014-08-18 18:00:14:
> Sorry, that link was just for reference, for when someone gets to implementing this feature.

Adding support for CORS requires changing the target x-ms-version to '2013-08-15', which turns this in a much bigger task than if the version stayed the same.  In every revision, there are potential breaking changes in the XML format sent/received to/from Azure.

From what I can see, the way metrics are represented in 2013-08-15 has changed: http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/azure/hh452235.aspx.

There are potentially more, we'll need to review the docs and run all the tests to find out.

To adapt StorageServiceProperties to return the new CORS properties, you'll need to make the data classes match the XML returned by Azure.  When there are lists involved, like in this case, it's a little more complex.  You can see an example of object models with lists in azure.servicemanagement.AffinityGroup, which contains StorageServices and HostedServices.

issue_title: CollectionManagement sample has an IDisposable that doesn't match it's docs
issue_body: If you look at [IDisposable](https://github.com/Azure/azure-documentdb-python/blob/1766aa9fc7cfc3b61c71248866e543a293595f0d/samples/CollectionManagement/Program.py#L49) in the ManageCollections sample you will notice it says that it's for use in a context manager to call a `close()` method. But if you look at the actual code there's no call to a `close()` method. I also didn't find a `close()` method on the `DocumentClient` in the SDK docs for the single use of `IDisposable` in the code. Lastly, in Python's stdlib there is [`contextlib.closing()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextlib.html#contextlib.closing) for this exact use-case.

issue_title: Mongo DB stores time as UTC although the timestamp corresponds to localtime
issue_body:
The Mongo DB observer takes the current time and stores it into the database to track when an experiment begun and when there was a last update.
However the time in local timezone is stored with UTC timezone (without calculating the UTC time).
When reading the database, one have to think about that he must not convert the UTC timestamp to the local time zone, but treat it as if it already was in local timezone.

The result is that if someone, let's say in GMT+2, runs an experiment and somebody else in GMT+1 reads the values, he sees that the last update of the experiment was in the future.

issue_title: Make it easier to extract a random FASTA subset, just by giving a target fraction
issue_body: The `randomSubset` filtering is fine, but we don't need that precision in some cases and it's a hassle to have to work out the number of reads. We could use reservoir sampling (which needs the sample size to be not too large, as the sample has to be held in memory). But simpler, it would be good to just say something like `sampleFraction=0.1` to filter approximately 10% of the reads. Then you can take similar (proportionally) samples without needing to care about the number of reads present.

issue_title: deprecate support for spark 1.2

issue_title: conda.install imports other conda modules
issue_body:
I just noted https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/install.py#L47,
however, it has always been a policy that `conda.install` has to be standalone, see:
https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/install.py#L22

The problem is that all our installers depend on this, that is, they use `conda.install` as a module for bootstrapping.  This means that currently (even though we can build installers) they are broken.

issue_title: Install of older python (revert) from newer yields error
issue_body:
Build log at https://travis-ci.org/conda/conda-build/jobs/160031372

2.7 and 3.5 builds complete just fine - but they start with (approximately) the python version that they are using for the testing.

Gist of the situation is that we start with a 3.5 installation, and then want to go back to a 3.4 installation for testing purposes.  With conda 4.1.11, this works.  For 4.2.6, it does not.  I have not dug in further, but would be happy to do so.

issue_title: Conda is installing unrequested feature
issue_body:
This seems to be a regression in `conda` 4.0.

I have created two test packages just to check this. They are empty and have just the `meta.yaml`:

test-release:
```yaml
package:
  name: test
  version: "1.0"

build:
  number: 0
  features:
```

test-debug:
```yaml
package:
  name: test
  version: "1.0"

build:
  number: 1
  features:
    - debug

requirements:
  run:
    - debug
```

(`debug` is the anaconda metapackage that only tracks the `debug` feature.)

Now, when creating a new environment with only the `test` package, it tries to install the `debug` version:

``sh
$ conda create -n test-pkg test=1.0
Fetching package metadata: ......
Solving package specifications: .........

Package plan for installation in environment D:\Miniconda\envs\test-pkg:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    debug: 1.0-0
    test:  1.0-debug_1 [debug]

Proceed ([y]/n)?
```

issue_title: Conda very slow after fresh reinstallation on Windows 10
issue_body:
## Current Behavior Hi guys, I know this topic might seem similar to some other ones with the same title however I would strongly argue it it different. Initially conda was fast in activating the environment and doing other basic commands like conda list. Over the last month it is super slow without me being a heavy user. I had very very slow responses in conda and that's why I decided to completely uninstall and reinstall it. Un-installation step (everything in anaconda prompt): 1) download anaconda-clean using the command `conda install anaconda-clean` 2)type in the anaconda prompt 'anaconda-clean --yes' ; this generated the backup file 3) restarted my pc to break any possible connections 4) ran the uninstaller found in Programns and Features in Control Panel 5) restart 6) removed all files (file explorer) that included the word conda or anaconda (including the backup file created above) 7) removed the environment variables that looked at any path that had the word anaconda or conda 8) restart 9) empty trash At this step I believe (or hope) that my pc is brand new and crystal clear (but won't be!). Re-installation: 1) downloaded and executed the 3.6 x64 .exe file (installation directory C:\anaconda\ -I created the anaconda folder) 2) restarted pc after installation finished Now the real party begins! I open up anaconda prompt and type 'conda info'. This will take 40sec to load!!! I create an new environment called py36env. I type 'activate py36env' and it takes 30 sec to load. This environment is totally empty as you can see below. When I type 'conda list' within the environment it literally takes 45 sec to load!! In an empty enivronment!! Apparently something is wrong and would really really appreciate any guidance! issue_title: Conda cripples itself on update
issue_body:
I have anaconda installed to /usr/local/anaconda3, I ran:

    conda update conda anaconda pandas numpy scipy statsmodels matplotlib

I thought everything worked fine, but then jupyterhub was behaving strangely, so I restarted it.. except it failed:

```
● jupyterhub.service - Jupyter Hub
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/local/anaconda3/etc/jupyter/../../../jupyter/jupyterhub.service; bad; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: failed (Result: exit-code) since Tue 2017-05-30 10:20:57 PDT; 33s ago
  Process: 31918 ExecStart=/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/jupyterhub -f /usr/local/anaconda3/etc/jupyter/jupyterhub_config.py (code=exited, status=1/FAILURE)
 Main PID: 31918 (code=exited, status=1/FAILURE)

May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server systemd[1]: Started Jupyter Hub.
May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server jupyterhub[31918]: Traceback (most recent call last):
May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server jupyterhub[31918]:   File "/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/jupyterhub", line 3, in <module>
May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server jupyterhub[31918]:     from jupyterhub.app import main
May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server jupyterhub[31918]: ImportError: No module named 'jupyterhub'
May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server systemd[1]: jupyterhub.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=1/FAILURE
May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server systemd[1]: jupyterhub.service: Unit entered failed state.
May 30 10:20:57 fraser-server systemd[1]: jupyterhub.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.
```

So I tried to `conda search jupyterhub` and got this:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/conda", line 4, in <module>
    import conda.cli
ImportError: No module named 'conda'
```

I confirmed that my PATH definitely set properly and the python is `/usr/local/anaconda3/bin/python`,  so I fired up python, but the welcome message was:

```
Python 3.5.2 (default, Nov  7 2016, 11:31:36)
[GCC 6.2.1 20160830] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>>
```

Which doesn't look like an anaconda python. I looked around in the `anaconda/bin` directory and everything looked fine.

My guess is that this is related to #2463 so I tried the 'fix' linked in that comment: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1733#issuecomment-150641779, basically reinstalling anaconda and then rsyncing in all the changes. That failed, as it overwrote the fixed files with the broken ones.

So at this point it looks like updating a few packages with conda destroyed the whole install and I am going to have to start from scratch, which is a little frustrating to say the least.

issue_title: Add self-check mode to the Miniconda installer
issue_body:
The miniconda installer already has code for checking that the binary is the right size and has the correct hash. This is useful to make sure that the download is correct.

However, you can not easily to tell that the Miniconda file downloaded is corrupt or the install failed. It would be good if Miniconda had a "self test" mode which you could run to check that the download worked.

Maybe a `./Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh --verify` or `./Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh --check`.

issue_title: Conflicting security-declarations
issue_body:
Moved from https://dev.plone.org/ticket/20289

Relates to https://github.com/plone/plone.app.blob/issues/22 which was fixed by david-batranu, but commentlessly reverted again.


Description
-----------

Since of Plone-4.3.5, rising an instance yields:

    2015-08-10 10:23:51 INFO ZServer HTTP server started at Mon Aug 10 10:23:51 2015
        Hostname: 0.0.0.0
        Port: 8080
    2015-08-10 10:23:53 WARNING SecurityInfo Conflicting security declarations for
    "<AccessControl.SecurityInfo.ClassSecurityInfo object at 0x5ddef10>"
    2015-08-10 10:23:53 WARNING SecurityInfo Conflicting security declarations for
    "<AccessControl.SecurityInfo.ClassSecurityInfo object at 0x5ddef10>"
    2015-08-10 10:23:53 WARNING SecurityInfo Class "BlobWrapper" had conflicting security declarations
    2015-08-10 10:23:53 WARNING SecurityInfo Conflicting security declarations for
    "<AccessControl.SecurityInfo.ClassSecurityInfo object at 0x5dd4690>"
    2015-08-10 10:23:53 WARNING SecurityInfo Conflicting security declarations for
    "<AccessControl.SecurityInfo.ClassSecurityInfo object at 0x5dd4690>"
    2015-08-10 10:23:53 WARNING SecurityInfo Class "BlobField" had conflicting security declarations


Observations
-----------------

- The warning has been introduced with plone.app.blob-1.5.11. in field.py, where the security-declarations were changed to use decorators, now. One can counterproof that, by simply copying field.py of vs. 1.5.10 over to 1.5.11.

- The history of plone.app.blob misses entirely entries for the time-range between the release of 1.5.10 and 1.5.11 _!!!_

- Version 1.5.12 then claims to be a "Rerelease for clarity because of double release of 1.5.11."

- There used to be the same warning-message concerning topics in the Plone-4.1-series, which was silenced – not fixed – by hannosch in: https://github.com/plone/Products.ATContentTypes/commit/1f88839d6bad753a25884d00b4eb4ce31c36dedd


Questions
--------------

- Why is the history of an entire version missing?

- Does double-release mean, there are two eggs existing, with the same version-number?

- How can one get a full list of all releases of an egg, on pypi's website e.g, or otherwise, to see the other half of the doubled release?

- Is silencing the warning considered to be an acceptable resolvement in this case?

- Is there possibly a guide, what needs to be regarded, when changing security-declas to use decorators?

- And of course: What could be a possible resolvement for the warning? I tried to mimicry hannosch's topics-fix in ATContentTypes/field.py, no luck.

Regards, Ida

issue_title: About convert cifar10 to a multipule device version(A TFoS app)
issue_body:
I successfully run the official mnist and cifar10 example with standalone mode.And now i want to change the cifar10 example to mult device and use cpus or gpus version like mnsit.
I refer to the official convert document but got many questions during the convert process.
Because of I mainly refer to the mnist example, so what I learn is 


this part 
*************************
  if job_name == "ps":
    server.join()
  elif job_name == "worker":
******************************
 is to divide the ps node and worker node ,the workers will go on do the tensorflow things.


this part:
***************************************************
 cluster, server = ctx.start_cluster_server(1, args.rdma)
***************************************************
is to start the cluster


this part is to define a supervisor
***************************************************
sv = tf.train.Supervisor(is_chief=(task_index == 0),
                               logdir=logdir,
                               init_op=init_op,…………………………
***************************************************


And here is my program:


# Copyright 2015 The TensorFlow Authors. All Rights Reserved.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
# ==============================================================================

"""A binary to train CIFAR-10 using a single GPU.

Accuracy:
cifar10_train.py achieves ~86% accuracy after 100K steps (256 epochs of
data) as judged by cifar10_eval.py.

Speed: With batch_size 128.

System        | Step Time (sec/batch)  |     Accuracy
------------------------------------------------------------------
1 Tesla K20m  | 0.35-0.60              | ~86% at 60K steps  (5 hours)
1 Tesla K40m  | 0.25-0.35              | ~86% at 100K steps (4 hours)

Usage:
Please see the tutorial and website for how to download the CIFAR-10
data set, compile the program and train the model.

http://tensorflow.org/tutorials/deep_cnn/
"""
from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division
from __future__ import print_function

from pyspark.context import SparkContext
from pyspark.conf import SparkConf
from tensorflowonspark import TFCluster, TFNode
from datetime import datetime

def print_log(worker_num, arg):
  print("{0}: {1}".format(worker_num, arg))


import os.path
import sys
import time

def main_fun(args, ctx):
  import tensorflow as tf
  import cifar10_model

  worker_num = ctx.worker_num
  job_name = ctx.job_name
  task_index = ctx.task_index
  if job_name == "ps":
    time.sleep((worker_num + 1) * 5)


  sys.argv = args
  FLAGS = tf.app.flags.FLAGS
  tf.app.flags.DEFINE_string('train_dir', '/tmp/cifar10_train',
                             """Directory where to write event logs """
                             """and checkpoint.""")
  tf.app.flags.DEFINE_integer('max_steps', 1000000,
                              """Number of batches to run.""")
  tf.app.flags.DEFINE_boolean('log_device_placement', False,
                              """Whether to log device placement.""")
  tf.app.flags.DEFINE_boolean('rdma', False, """Whether to use rdma.""")

  # cifar10_model.maybe_download_and_extract()
  if tf.gfile.Exists(FLAGS.train_dir):
    tf.gfile.DeleteRecursively(FLAGS.train_dir)
  tf.gfile.MakeDirs(FLAGS.train_dir)

  cluster, server = TFNode.start_cluster_server(ctx, 1, FLAGS.rdma)
  if job_name == "ps":
    server.join()
  elif job_name == "worker":
  # Train CIFAR-10 for a number of steps.
      with tf.device(tf.train.replica_device_setter(
              worker_device="/job:worker/task:%d" % task_index,
              cluster=cluster)):
        global_step = tf.contrib.framework.get_or_create_global_step()

        # Get images and labels for CIFAR-10.
        images, labels = cifar10_model.distorted_inputs()

        # Build a Graph that computes the logits predictions from the
        # inference model.
        logits = cifar10_model.inference(images)

        # Calculate loss.
        loss = cifar10_model.loss(logits, labels)

        init_op = tf.global_variables_initializer()
        summary_op = tf.summary.merge_all()
        saver = tf.train.Saver()
        # Build a Graph that trains the model with one batch of examples and
        # updates the model parameters.
        train_op = cifar10_model.train(loss, global_step)
        logdir = FLAGS.train_dir
        print("tensorflow model path: {0}".format(logdir))

        if job_name == "worker" and task_index == 0:
            summary_writer = tf.summary.FileWriter(logdir, graph=tf.get_default_graph())


            class _LoggerHook(tf.train.SessionRunHook):
              """Logs loss and runtime."""

              def begin(self):
                self._step = -1

              def before_run(self, run_context):
                self._step += 1
                self._start_time = time.time()
                return tf.train.SessionRunArgs(loss)  # Asks for loss value.

              def after_run(self, run_context, run_values):
                duration = time.time() - self._start_time
                loss_value = run_values.results
                if self._step % 10 == 0:
                  num_examples_per_step = FLAGS.batch_size
                  examples_per_sec = num_examples_per_step / duration
                  sec_per_batch = float(duration)

                  format_str = ('%s: step %d, loss = %.2f (%.1f examples/sec; %.3f '
                                'sec/batch)')
                  print (format_str % (datetime.now(), self._step, loss_value,
                                       examples_per_sec, sec_per_batch))

            with tf.train.MonitoredTrainingSession(
                checkpoint_dir=FLAGS.train_dir,
                hooks=[tf.train.StopAtStepHook(last_step=FLAGS.max_steps),
                       tf.train.NanTensorHook(loss),
                       _LoggerHook()],
                config=tf.ConfigProto(
                    log_device_placement=FLAGS.log_device_placement)) as mon_sess:
              while not mon_sess.should_stop():
                mon_sess.run(train_op)
        print("{0} stopping supervisor".format(datetime.now().isoformat()))



if __name__ == '__main__':
  sc = SparkContext(conf=SparkConf().setAppName("cifar10_train"))
  num_executors = int(sc._conf.get("spark.executor.instances"))
  num_ps = 0

  cluster = TFCluster.run(sc, main_fun, sys.args, num_executors, num_ps, False, TFCluster.InputMode.TENSORFLOW)
  cluster.shutdown()





The program above is just like TFoS cifar10_train.
It not divide the job.It just give the job to one slaver.
So here is my queshion:


1.In cifar10 example of TFoS，I found two variables are not used.cluster_spec and server，
which mnist example used 。how to use this two variables in cifar10? I default used them as mnist and
change the code structure like the program show.

2.Is the supervisor is the key part of a TFOS app(I mean to divide the jobs in to many portions)
Because my program just overlook the supervisor.I have added it ,but the parameter in

tf.train.Supervisor()

are not so common, so I overlook .As the result of the program shows , it`s not a multiple device app


3.Is there any good opening source program I can refer to except the official examples？
Or would @ilia14 you mind sharing your convertion of the cifar10. I saw you manage done it in #129

4.To complete my work in time(my work is complete a program based on cifar10 but read jpg or png and train on TFoS),I need to convert my dataset to mnist type（because I have only the mnist example to use） ,but I googled much times but can`t find one program to do this.Does any one have recommendation？


Thanks a lot！！

issue_title: about shutdown
issue_body:
hi @leewyang
we used tfos running well. thanks for contribution.

after inspecting the code, one point puzzled me.

[L160](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/tensorflowonspark/TFSparkNode.py#L160) each worker/executor records its `executor_id` in file on local working directory.

`nodeRDD = sc.parallelize(range(num_executors), num_executors)`
using nodeRDD to run the tf cluster.

while shutdown
[L497](https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/tensorflowonspark/TFSparkNode.py#L497)
here start

`workerRDD = self.sc.parallelize(range(workers), workers)`

1. how does it make sure that the `workerRDD` will read file in the same directory as `nodeRDD`? each partition in workerRDD will read its own local executor_id, but it might be a new executor which is different from all executors of nodeRDD, and no executor_id file exist.
2. shutdown the ps is reasonable, however, since the worker will finished no matter in mode.SPARK or mode.TENSORFLOW, why need to shutdown the workers?

thanks

issue_title: Patch for generating xml:base attribute in RDF/XML output
issue_body:
gklyn...@gmail.com, 2011-09-23T14:54:59.000Z
<pre>
I have a need to be able to generate XML/RDF output with a specified xml:base value.

Attached is a patch that implements this for the XMLSerializer and PrettyXMLSerializer modules, with an extra test case forPrettyXMLSerializer, and a new test module for XMLSerializer.

test_serializexml.py is pretty much a copy of the test_prettyxml.py, with the tests tweaked to match the output generated.

Apart from the test cases, the patch contains no documentation for how to use this - I wasn't sure where that should go.  (xml:base is generated by adding an xml_base=... keyword argument to the serialize call.  There is no attempt to align the value with the base provided: my application uses different values for these, as I use a relative base URI.)

issue_title: Pypi上aliyun-python-sdk-core-v3的代码和Github代码不一致
issue_body:
目前Pypi上的aliyun-python-sdk-core-v3最新版本是2.8.9，使用时会出现错误：
```
aliyunsdkcore.acs_exception.exceptions.ClientException: SDK.InvalidRegionId Can not find endpoint to access.
```
调查后发现aliyun-python-sdk-core-v3中 client.py 文件中`_resolve_endpoint`和Github上的内容不一样：

Pypi上的版本：
```
    def _resolve_endpoint(self, request):
        return endpoint_resolver.resolve_endpoint(self.__region_id, request, self._location_service)
```

Github上版本：
```
    def _resolve_endpoint(self, request):

        if isinstance(request, CommonRequest) and request.get_domain():
            return request.get_domain()

        endpoint = None
        if request.get_location_service_code() is not None:
            endpoint = self._location_service.find_product_domain(
                self.get_region_id(), request.get_location_service_code(), request.get_product(),
                request.get_location_endpoint_type())
        if endpoint is None:
            endpoint = region_provider.find_product_domain(
                self.get_region_id(), request.get_product())
            if endpoint is None:
                raise ClientException(
                    error_code.SDK_INVALID_REGION_ID,
                    error_msg.get_msg('SDK_INVALID_REGION_ID'))
            if not isinstance(request, AcsRequest):
                raise ClientException(
                    error_code.SDK_INVALID_REQUEST,
                    error_msg.get_msg('SDK_INVALID_REQUEST'))
        return endpoint
```

应该是没有更新Pypi上的代码，希望可以尽快解决。

issue_title: Mxnet R Package Installation Doc Bug
issue_body:
Wrong:

```
# current repo doc:  https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/tree/master/R-package
cran <- getOption("repos")
cran["dmlc"] <- "https://apache-mxnet.s3-accelerate.dualstack.amazonaws.com/R/CRAN/"
options(repos = cran)
install.packages("mxnet",dependencies = T)
```

Correct:
```
cran <- getOption("repos")
cran["dmlc"] <- "https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/apache-mxnet/R/CRAN/"
options(repos = cran)
install.packages("mxnet",dependencies = T)
```

## Reference

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/43872455/mxnet-package-installation-in-r

issue_title: Neural Style in python implementation
issue_body:
I'm seeing some issues in the updated [neural style](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/tree/master/example/neural-style), namely: 1. The target_shape has been deprecated. Shape should be used instead. See style_gram_symbol() on line 106:
`x = mx.sym.Reshape(style[i], target_shape=(int(shape[1]), int(np.prod(shape[2:]))))` should be changed to:
`x = mx.sym.Reshape(style[i], shape=(int(shape[1]), int(np.prod(shape[2:]))))` 2. What is grad_scale? It is using 'np.prod(shape[1:] \* shape[1]', shouldn't this be 'np.prod(shape[1:] **2)' ??? The terms should be N^2 \* M^2? 3. Where are the constants on the style_loss? According to the paper, loss = 1 / (4 \* N^2 \* M^2) \* sum((G - A)^2). I only see the the sum of the squared difference in the get_loss function. 4. Is this using pre-normalized VGG-net weights? If not, shouldn't L1 normalization happen on both style and content gradients? This tends to give much better results and has been incorporated into https://github.com/jcjohnson/neural-style. 5. Is using SGD appropriate? L-BFGS would be best to compare to the original author, but I haven't seen that implemented (another issue perhaps?) in MXNet. The second alternative would be ADAM, which is currently working. I'm not an expert, so I would love to hear some thoughts on these.

## Description
This issue is to track the comments from the MXNet 1.0.0 vote thread. Refer to [link 1](https://mail-archives.apache.org/mod_mbox/incubator-general/201712.mbox/%3C7D5FA7AB-8172-4927-B34C-16281F62CAC2%40classsoftware.com%3E) and [link 2](https://mail-archives.apache.org/mod_mbox/incubator-general/201712.mbox/%3CCAK1xzDeBiNqcK_nZ4wSYehrLRtxESWRpPoZm8dg6UYDt6rNevA%40mail.gmail.com%3E) for details.

The comments mentioned are as follows - 

- **Issue 1:** LICENSE has a number of minor issues (LICENSE must be reviewed in addition to issues below)

- **Issue 2:** this file has an ASF header looks to be BSD licensed. [1] Other files that are licensed in similar confusing ways include [2][3][4][14]

- **Issue 3:** same with with MIT licensed files. [9][18][19]

- **Issue 4:** this file is BSD licensed [5] so would need to be added to LICENSE. As are a dozen files here [6] and this file [13, and this one [14] and 6 files here [15] and this one [16] and also this one [23]

- **Issue 5:** some file are still missing headers. For instance [7] and probable more.

- **Issue 6:** This file is listed at Apache 2.0 licensed  in the LICNSE but is actually MIT licensed. [8] As I think is this [10] and these [17][20] (or it should be mentioned in license)

- **Issue 7:** This file has two apache headers [22]

- **Issue 8:** Also there one outstanding question from the last release how is this file licensed? (if you follow the URL mentioned in the file it is unclear). [24] but it unclear of the code come form that site or was written for the project by the author of that site.

## Error Message:
For the following input array
```
y = [[3,3],[4,4],[5,5]]
z = [[6,6],[7,7],[8,8]]
```
The output of the concat function from the symbol API with axis=1 is
```
[[[ 3.  3.]
  [ 4.  4.]
  [ 5.  5.]
  [ 6.  6.]
  [ 7.  7.]
  [ 8.  8.]]]
```
But the expected output is 
```
[[ 3.  3.  6.  6.]
 [ 4.  4.  7.  7.]
 [ 5.  5.  8.  8.]]
```

## Minimum reproducible example
```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import nd
from collections import namedtuple
import numpy as np

y = mx.sym.Variable('y')
z = mx.sym.Variable('z')
net = mx.sym.concat(*[y,z], dim=1)

y = np.array([[3,3],[4,4],[5,5]])
z = np.array([[6,6],[7,7],[8,8]])

inputs = [y,z]
data_names = ['y','z']
data_shapes = []

for idx, input_name in enumerate(data_names):
    batch_size = 1
    data_shapes.append((input_name, inputs[idx].shape))
mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=net, context=mx.cpu(), data_names=data_names, label_names=None)
mod.bind(for_training=False, data_shapes=data_shapes, label_shapes=None)
mod.init_params()
batch = namedtuple('Batch', ['data'])

data_forward = []
for idx, input_name in enumerate(data_names):
    val = inputs[idx]
    data_forward.append(mx.nd.array([val]))
print(data_forward)
    
print("\n\n**Concat Output** from symbol API")
mod.forward(batch(data_forward))
result = mod.get_outputs()[0]
print(result)# default axis
```
NDArray function call
```python
y = np.array([[3,3],[4,4],[5,5]])
z = np.array([[6,6],[7,7],[8,8]])

y = nd.array(y)
z = nd.array(z)
nd.concat(y,z,dim=1)
```

## Steps to reproduce
1. Run the above code sample.

issue_title: Supporting graceful worker exits (alternatives to CELERYD_MAX_TASKS_PER_CHILD), eg for leaky workers
issue_body:
Hi,

I was wondering if it would be possible to have a custom hook or a special exception class that would allow a worker to decide at runtime that it should not continue.

The application is to allow workers which detect they've allocated too much memory since they were started to exit gracefully.  I find this a much more useful measure than the crude "max requests per child" approach, and keeps system memory useful and active.  Currently I'm just raising ``SystemExit()``, which seems to be interpreted as an error by billiard, making it raise ``WorkerLostError``.

If you've got no objection, please let me know how I can help!

Cheers,
Sam.

issue_title: Documentation on filters and name needs update
issue_body:
The docs for `containers` and `images` could use an entry for the `filters` parameter, which appears to be passed a dictionary that corresponds to the filters available via the docker command line

Also, the `name` parameter for `images(...)` could use some clarification that it is an exact search only e.g. if the image name+tag is `foo/bar:12.04`, then only the string `foo/bar` will return this container, and not any substrings e.g. `foo`, `bar`, or `foo/bar:12.04`

issue_title: OO API?
issue_body:
Hello,

The API currently returns a dict from many calls where it seems, perhaps naively, an object would be more useful. I'm aiming to add some OO notions to the API exposed by docker-py, but not before consulting people here.

For example, Client.create_container returns a dict, and usage looks like:

```
client = Client()
cont = client.create_container(**info)
if 'Id' in cont:
    client.start(cont['Id'])
```

with needing both `client` and `cont` for the 'start' call. Whereas if create_container returned a hypothetical Container object, the container run code then look like:

```
client = Client()
cont = client.create_container(**info)
cont.start()
```

There's a [branch called 'objects'](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/tree/objects) that looks like the very beginnings of such an approach, but it hasn't been touched in 3 years, and current master shows no sign of incorporating any OO notions for the API, outside of docker.Client.

Why that approach was dropped? I'd like to look at adding some objects to the API, but not if there were unforeseen issues with that. If there are issues, it would be preferable to have them written down and documented here. (There are various ways to approach supporting legacy code, but if an OO api is fundamentally incisompatible, for whatever reason, then discussing support of legacy code seems premature.)

I was unable to find any docs or issues relating to this particular architectural decision, though I did come across #417 and #538, which aren't this specific question.

issue_title: Unable to install latest x64 sdk on Debian Buster
issue_body:
I wrote a simple series of steps to update to new available version of .NET Core. I am on Debian Buster (Debian 10).

**Remove old version**

    cd
    sudo rm -rf dotnet
    sudo rm -rf /usr/local/bin/dotnet 
    rm -rf .dotnet/
    rm -rf .templateengine/
    rm -rf .nuget/
    rm -rf dotnet.tar.gz
    sudo updatedb

**Install current version**

    curl -sSL -o dotnet.tar.gz https://aka.ms/dotnet-sdk-2.0.0-linux-x64
    mkdir -p ~/dotnet && tar zxf dotnet.tar.gz -C ~/dotnet
    export PATH=$PATH:$HOME/dotnet

issue_title: setup.py installs Python scripts as 'data' rather than 'scripts'
issue_body:
```
The setup.py script lists the Python script as a 'data file', alike:

      data_files=[("bin", ["gflags2man.py"])],

while it should be a 'script' instead:

      scripts=["gflags2man.py"],

Installing it a script will make sure that distutils correctly update the 
Python shebang and make the script executable.

I'm attaching a patch fixing setup.py.
```

issue_title: mapbox returns driving instead of walking directions
issue_body:
I'm currently testing mapbox results for walking directions from one location to a public transportation stop/station in the Berlin area (Germany). However, mapbox returns only driving directions. The question is, if I'm doing something wrong, or if mapbox is simply incapable of handling such a request.

These are the commands:
```
MB_DIRECTIONS=$(mapbox directions "[$CURRENT_LONG, $CURRENT_LAT]" "[$STATION_LONG, $STATION_LAT]" --profile mapbox.walking --alternatives)
MB_INSTRUCTIONS=$(echo "$MB_DIRECTIONS" | jq '.routes[0].steps[] .maneuver.instruction' -M -r)
MB_DISTANCES=$(echo "$MB_DIRECTIONS" | jq '.routes[0].steps[] .distance' -M -r)
```
Variables for current lat/long and station lat/long are defined earlier.

This is the mapbox result (note the difference between linear and "walking" distance):
```
From: Herbert-von-Karajan-Straße 1, Berlin, Berlin 10785, Germany [52.510032,13.369693]
To: Potsdamer Straße, Berlin, Berlin 10785, Germany [52.507568,13.369186]

109	 	Head right
40		Turn right onto Tiergartenstraße (L 1137) at the end of the road
216		Turn right onto Ben-Gurion-Straße

Linear Distance: 275.9384 Meters

```

This is the (accurate) result in Apple Maps:
http://i.imgur.com/zLhpMQI.png

issue_title: Can't tokenize alias as key without seperator
issue_body:
js-yaml: @3.7.0
Node v7.2.1
Windows 10 Pro

*disclaimer: I'm not an expert on the YAML spec*

### the bug
"`:`" is a valid character when defining anchors and using aliases.
It is valid to use aliases as keys.

"`:`" is also used to denote a node.
This seems to cause a bug where you are unable to use aliases as keys unless you separate the alias from the "`:`" with whitespace.

Trailing "`:`"'s look like they're being included in the alias token by the while loop in [`loader.readAlias()`](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/blob/master/lib/js-yaml/loader.js#L1233).
I don't know the code very well, but a fix might need some extra logic in [`loader.composeNode()`](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/blob/master/lib/js-yaml/loader.js#L1338).

### some tests
```js
const tests = [
  // working cases  (space before `:`)
  `
    adjective: &adj  stealthy!
    *adj :           box :)
  `,
  `
    adjective: &adj: stealthy!
    *adj: :          box :)
  `,
  `
    adjective: &:    stealthy!
    *: :             box :)
  `,
  // broken cases  (no space)
  `
    adjective: &adj  stealthy!
    *adj:            box :)
  `,
  `
    adjective: &adj: stealthy!
    *adj::           box :)
  `,
  `
    adjective: &:    stealthy!
    *:               box :)
  `,
]
for ( const content of tests )
  try{ console.log(require('js-yaml').load(content)) }
  catch(e) { console.log(e) }
```
### output
```bash
{ adjective: 'stealthy!', 'stealthy!': 'box :)' }
{ adjective: 'stealthy!', 'stealthy!': 'box :)' }
{ adjective: 'stealthy!', 'stealthy!': 'box :)' }
{ Error
    at generateError (C:\Users\leigh\repos\puppet\test-site\data\node_modules\js-yaml\lib\js-yaml\loader.js:162:10)
    at throwError (C:\Users\leigh\repos\puppet\test-site\data\node_modules\js-yaml\lib\js-yaml\loader.js:168:9)
    at readAlias (C:\Users\leigh\repos\puppet\test-site\data\node_modules\js-yaml\lib\js-yaml\loader.js:1244:5)
    at composeNode (C:\Users\leigh\repos\puppet\test-site\data\node_modules\js-yaml\lib\js-yaml\loader.js:1336:20)
    at readBlockMapping (C:\Users\leigh\repos\puppet\test-site\data\node_modules\js-yaml\lib\js-yaml\loader.js:1004:16)

issue_title: YAML 1.2 spec: Example 8.2. Block Indentation Indicator
issue_body:
When I try to parse example 8.2 at http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#Block
I got:
```
[ 'detected\n',
  '\n\n# detected\n',
  '\n explicit\n',
  '\n\t\ndetected\n' ]
```
But according to the canonical form last two lines must not get a leading '\n':
```
%YAML 1.2
---
!!seq [
  !!str "detected\n",
  !!str "\n\n# detected\n",
  !!str "·explicit\n",
  !!str "\t·detected\n",
]
```

issue_title: Resources from `.get` and refresh interact poorly
issue_body:
`refreshSourceIterator` constructs goal states for every resource in your snapshot. If any of them are marked `External`, `refreshSourceIterator` drops the `External` bit before sending the event to the engine. The end result is that resources that were `External` get replaced and become non-`External`, which can cause engine crashes later on.

The engine should be smart about this and refresh an `External` resource by reading it again.

issue_title: Option to specify no_cover in config file on per-target basis
issue_body: Right now if you don't want to use the default cover file for a target, you have to specify the `--no_cover` option on the commandline every time you build. It should be possible to add a `no_cover` flag to a target in the config file in case you don't want that target to use the default cover page. There are good reasons you may want to do this (maybe you want to include a special cover page for that target or you want something to appear before the table of contents that's normally provided by the cover page)

Using pipdeptree however, we can see that stripe specifies the `requests` dependency twice:

```
  - stripe [required: >=1.53.0, installed: 2.0.1]
    - requests [required: >=2, installed: 2.19.1]
      - certifi [required: >=2017.4.17, installed: 2018.4.16]
      - chardet [required: >=3.0.2,<3.1.0, installed: 3.0.4]
      - idna [required: >=2.5,<2.8, installed: 2.7]
      - urllib3 [required: >=1.21.1,<1.24, installed: 1.23]
    - requests [required: >=2, installed: 2.19.1]
      - certifi [required: >=2017.4.17, installed: 2018.4.16]
      - chardet [required: >=3.0.2,<3.1.0, installed: 3.0.4]
      - idna [required: >=2.5,<2.8, installed: 2.7]
      - urllib3 [required: >=1.21.1,<1.24, installed: 1.23]
```

Currently, `setup.py` [defines requests twice as a dependency](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/66aa334145d7a702fea03473911a8777abbbb60c/setup.py#L47-L48). Once plain, once with the `[security]` extra package.

With this information, I found a relevant upstream pip bug: pypa/pip#4957

The duplicate-dependency issue is easily worked around by making plain requests only a dependency on python_version >= 3.0. I believe this will fix the resulting issue as well. I'll file a PR.

issue_title: Output DAG with status information

issue_title: "cannot decode v0.3 services payload" error when tracing aiopg
issue_body:
I have a Python application using aiopg and monitored by Datadog. When starting, it sends a bad frame to `trace-agent` and then everything goes fine.

Versions:
- datadog-agent: 6.2.1
- ddtrace-py: 0.12.1
- aiopg: 0.14.0

Here is a minimalist test case:

```python
import asyncio
import aiopg

from ddtrace import patch

patch(aiopg=True)


async def main():
    async with aiopg.connect(host=None) as db:
        pass

    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

This logs the following error:
```
failed_to_send services to Agent: HTTP error status 400, reason Bad Request, message Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
Date: Mon, 18 Jun 2018 15:25:18 GMT
Content-Length: 59
```

And then `trace-agent` reports:
```
trace-agent[4437]: 2018-06-18 15:31:16 ERROR (receiver.go:275) - cannot decode v0.3 services payload: msgp: attempted to decode type "nil" with method for "str"
```

I believe this is related to https://github.com/DataDog/datadog-trace-agent/issues/350.

I would like to use pykafka to connect to IBM Bluemix Message Hub with is a Kafka implementation.  There is a kafka-python example in this thread.  https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35294949/can-i-call-the-bluemix-message-hub-service-from-python/40325181#40325181

A new Python client

Confluent, the company founded by the creators of Kafka, has released a Python client (https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python). It is built as a thin bindings layer on top of librdkafka and works for both Python 2 and 3.

To use with Message Hub, simply pass these properties in when creating a Producer/Consumer:

    'ssl.ca.location': '/etc/ssl/certs/',
    'sasl.mechanisms': 'PLAIN',

    'sasl.password': '<PASSWORD>',

Note: The value of ‘ssl.ca.location depends’ on your operating system. For example:

issue_title: Snappy Compression is (probaby) broken
issue_body: [This commit](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/commit/92f7460784cd38e38a793db3188ece41bc8f085e) stopped us from stripping snappy headers, but that only works part of the time.  [This](https://github.com/mumrah/kafka-python/pull/127/files) Pull Request has us thinking that our snappy implementation is deficient. issue_title: Support automatic api version discovery

issue_title: Virtualenv Python Pycurl Issue
issue_body:
When trying to deploy pycurl to a virtualenv via the vitrualenvs/requirement.txt file, pycurl decides to ignore the fact that we are attempting to install it into a virtualenv.

Since it appears that the PYTHONPATH or sys.path is not including the system packages, when the virtualenv is activated, pycurl imports fail.

This is a known issue with the way that pycurl is installed within a virtualenv, outside of the streamparse project. My team has used a workaround (manual) to get this fixed, copying the pycurl.so file into the virtualenv's site-packages.

There should be a way developed for doing pre/post scripting in the virtualenv setup.

issue_title: Blanket X-Frame-Options SAMEORIGIN in Apache shared config
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version): Debian 9.3 Stretch ## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc): apt, version 0.10.2 is installed ## Issue: On my system, Certbot installed an Apache configuration file in `/etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-apache.conf` that is included in all the automatically generated sites config. It features the following line: `Header always set X-Frame-Options SAMEORIGIN`. It sort of makes sense to harden the web server on top of using HTTPS. ## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because: It breaks WordPress OEmbed feature. OEmbed allows websites to embed a HTML snippet to display a nice render of a blog post. However, the WordPress snippet includes an iframe to an internal embed URL that can't be displayed on third-party websites if X-Frame-Options SAMEORIGIN is set for all requests. Example WordPress embed snippet: ```html
Reporters on icebergs while DC tells Minnesota to ‘stuff’ our weather expertise
``` However, [the 0.10.x version of the options-ssl-apache.conf](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/0.10.x/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/options-ssl-apache.conf) doesn't show this line, so I was wondering who could have made the change and how to prevent it. I was able to comment out this line on my own server, but I can't do it on `blogs.mprnews.org` for example, which breaks their embeds without their knowledge.

issue_title: OCSP stapling in Nginx
issue_body:
Currently we always enable OCSP stapling and do so when deploying the certificate. There's an enhancements system in Certbot which can be used to request/enable OCSP stapling, but the Nginx plugin ignores that entirely.

In the short term (for 0.9.0), I think we should remove OSCP stapling from the Nginx plugin. In the long term, we should make sure it properly uses the enhancements system and either:
* Make OCSP stapling the default in Certbot
* Only enable OCSP stapling in Nginx when requested by the user

I'm not sure if this is a dupe of #2446 , which has no description

issue_title: Rewrite our Dockerfile
issue_body:
Our current Dockerfile was largely written roughly 6 months before Let's Encrypt entered beta and it was a convenient way to test the project on different systems. It's in need of an update. I propose we update our Dockerfile to do the following:

* Continue installing from our repo rather than OS packages
* Remove unneeded OS dependencies after installing Certbot (such as `gcc`?)
* Clean `pip` cache after installing `certbot` (or don't use it all)
* Don't install the Apache or Nginx plugins

The resulting image should be roughly the size if we simply installed the `certbot` OS package. If this isn't possible for some reason, we may have to choose a different approach.

issue_title: Add Synced Flush Support
issue_body:
With Elasticsearch 1.6, a new API and recovery tool called "[Synced Flush](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/1.6/indices-synced-flush.html)" will appear. This will work similar to an optimized index so that indices can be recovered almost instantly, without having to compare segment files. However, the added benefit is that the older indices do _not_ need to be optimized at all; they just need to stop writing.

**Note**: For anyone that happens across this feature request. Optimizing an index is superior to synced flush in that the optimized version will get [sometimes significantly] better search performance, as well as reduced memory.

It would be great if sync flush could be triggered by Curator by having it as an option identical to `optimize`, minus optimize-specific flags (like the number of segments).

```
curator sync-flush indices <<index selection parameters>>
```

It may even be very useful to trigger this before (and perhaps after?) an optimize is run. Optimize is different in that it is just a forced merge that doesn't necessarily imply read-only indices (certainly that's the intent with merging to 1 segment), but synced flushing is meant for read-only (even if just temporary) indices for speedy recoveries. As a result, synced flushes will fail if the shard is being actively written, but the cost is very low.

issue_title: Tracking Element Changes
issue_body:
I did a bit of thinking on how to track element changes in things like lists and dicts. I came up with [spectate](https://github.com/rmorshea/spectate). It has a few limitations, and might be a bit hacky, but it allows you to create classes whose instances have tracked methods.

The instances of a `WatchedList` (as in this [example](https://github.com/rmorshea/spectate/blob/master/examples/basic.ipynb)) would tell you when their items are changed:

```python
WatchedList = WatchedType('WatchedList', list, ListSpectator, '__setitem__')
wl = WatchedList([1, 2, 3])

# will notify when
# an item changes
wl[0] = 2
```
```python
# the spectator will print this
'__setitem__ : {0: 1} -> {0: 2}'
```

+ alternative approach: #278

issue_title: using traitlets.config.get_config() in python config file breaks load_config_file
issue_body: I put these lines at the top of a python config file: from traitlets.config import get_config c = get_config() I did this so my editor would stop bugging me about c or get_config not being defined. Unfortunately, this subtly broke load_config_file, because that sets up global with `get_config` and `c`, both of which I just accidentally destroyed. I suggest that in the comments that are automatically generated when generate_config_file is used, a very explicit warning be placed to guard against this. Or perhaps there's some way to check if get_config or c has been mangled/modified accidentally

issue_title: Global exempt_method to OPTIONS not working
issue_body:
I'm trying to allow CORS requests from any user, without any authentication. Thus I intend to use the `exempt_methods` parameter of `FalconAuthMiddleware`, as:

```
auth = TokenAuthBackend(user_loader=find_user)
auth_middleware = FalconAuthMiddleware(auth, exempt_methods=['HEAD',
                                                             'OPTIONS'])
```

But this is not working, I still unable to make requests from the browser without getting a 401 status code. The only way I managed to do it was overriding Authentication for a specific resource, with something like:

```
class FooResource(BaseResource):
    auth = {
        'exempt_methods': ['OPTIONS']
    }
    ...
```
This is not a elegant solution because I'll need to set `auth.exempt_methods` in every resource.
Any idea what is going on here? Thanks in advance!

issue_title: DeepSpeech binary only shoud be linked against sox
issue_body:
Right now, we include include and linker flags for `sox` when we build everything, including node and python packages. This results in OSX on a useless `libsox.3.dylib` dep for example.

Use of `sox` is limited to `client.cc`/`deepspeech` binary, so the flags should not be there always.

the RegionFactory looks at hardwired location for the source files, so currently it's impossible to use regions from `nupic.regions.PyRegion` and an alternative library/path, eg `nupicvision.regions.ImageSensor` 

workaround is the files have to be copied over to nupic location.

issue_title: Proposal for some UI/UX improvements in the replay banner
issue_body:
![banner](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2514780/43408616-ccce3a80-93ef-11e8-98f9-e74e042364a6.gif)

In #462 @ibnesayeed made some **_great_** updates to the ipwb replay banner to vastly improve its aesthetics and functionality, but I think there are a few things we can do to make it more useable. I am hoping we can use this ticket for discussing particular improvements. Below I have provided an initial set.

- [ ] Highlight forward/back buttons on hover to provide an additional selected context -- redundant of the pointer icon but visually useful.
- [ ] Dim forward/backward buttons when not clickable (currently just indicated by no-pointer on hover)
- [ ] Prevent sone series of clicks from causing the UI elements to appear "selected" (see GIF)
- [ ] Provide some indication with the human-readable date that a hover will reveal the more specific datetime
- [ ] Change the Reconstruct icon to ipwb logo
- [ ] Add some event (go to ipwb replay home?) when the banner logo is clicked

issue_title: ToPILImage assumes RGB for 3 channel ndarrays
issue_body:
I think the `ToPILImage` transform is great however, looking internally it seems it makes some assumptions about color spaces. i.e. if you pass a 3-channel ndarray to the transform it loads it tells PIL that this should be interpreted as `RGB`, however it could easily be `YCbCr`, `HSV` or `LAB` (these are the 3 channel formats [supported by PIL](http://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/3.4.x/handbook/concepts.html#concept-modes))

Additionally, there is [a PR](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/189/files) to add `RGBA` support, assuming 4 channel ndarrays are `RGBA`, whereas PIL also supports `CMYK`.

Perhaps we should change the transform slightly, by allowing the `__init__` function to accept the image mode it should convert to (where the mode is a PIL `Image` mode)? Happy to send a PR for this if you think this is a good idea

issue_title: division by zero in make_grid
issue_body:
I occasionally see a division by zero from the following line.

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/704404928d7ec610add4ea40f36bb1e74f899d91/torchvision/utils.py#L55

This is actually not surprising since `min` can be equal to `max` if all values are the same. However, what is surprising that it only seems to happen with CUDA tensors.

``` python
torchvision.utils.make_grid(tensor=torch.zeros(2, 3, 4, 4), normalize=True) # works
torchvision.utils.make_grid(tensor=torch.zeros(2, 3, 4, 4).cuda(), normalize=True) # fails
```

issue_title: Incorrect documentation for error handling
issue_body:
HI, In the handling errors section of https://docs.recurly.com/client-libraries/python it reads: except ValidationError, errors:
for e in errors.itervalues(): Which raises an error that errors has no itervalues. I think it should read: except recurly.ValidationError, vadlidation_error:
for e in vadlidation_error.errors.itervalues(): Sorry about the weird line breaks :( I couldn't get it to look nice... ~Victor

issue_title: Invert Y axis of joystick
issue_body:
I'm wondering if there's an easy way to invert the 'Y' axes values of the joysticks in the simulator automatically, because a value of 1 in the simulator intuitively should make the robot move 'forward', but because most joysticks have negative 1 at the top, teams negate this value in code, so the robot moves backward. 

I've created a hot fix by adding jaxes[1]*=-1  in ui.py

issue_title: Annoy less resilient to id holes than before
issue_body:
Hey @erikbern, 

TL;DR: `1.9.4` is a lot less robust to holes in the id space than `1.3.2`.

--

After upgrading annoy to `1.9.4` for DW, we got a pretty significant drop in metrics. 

After investigations, it seems that compared to older versions of annoy, the latest version is not very robust to holes in the IDs (which we have for DW because of some multistage filtering logic). 

In an experiment where I compared building a tree without holes (id: 0 to n-1) with 30% of holes in the ids, I found that annoy `1.3.2` got a 3% drop in perf (cos sim of nn) where annoy `1.9.4` perf dropped by 36%.

Here are the numbers for mean cos sim of a bunch of nn queries:
```
	old_annoy	new_annoy	old_holes_annoy	new_holes_annoy
count	5000.000000	5000.000000	3499.000000	3520.000000
mean	0.135056	0.132766	0.140376	0.180412
std	0.013602	0.013103	0.017448	0.017294

25%	0.125277	0.123374	0.128233	0.168452

(If you want the code to reproduce this I can open source it)

Were you aware of this issue?

issue_title: FTP RemoteTarget tries to create local temp file in root
issue_body:
luigi.contrib.ftp.RemoteTarget open('r') tries to create a local temporary file based on the remote file path with a random suffix but using the entire path of the remote file.

In my case, the remote file is in the root folder of the FTP server so it tries to create the same file locally with '-luigi-tmp-%09d' as a suffix.  Because I don't have write permission to my root folder it fails.

issue_title: Execution summary breaks for unhashable parameters
issue_body:
The newly added `execution_summary` module in luigi 2.0 doesn't seem to work nicely with unhashable parameters, and running a task with any such parameter will raise a TypeError. It seems that the use of sets of parameters in the `_get_str` method is the cause of this error.

I'm not entirely sure what the logic is regarding that internal function, but is there a way of producing the execution summary without the use of sets?

issue_title: Cannot run luigi on AWS Lambda

Set the basePath to '/' in the swagger definition.

issue_title: update the inspector components/widget to the new format
Incorrectly raised Error in part.instance()
issue_body:
The code looks like this: 

```
    def instance(self):
        """
        Retrieve the single (expected) instance of this 'Part' (of `Category.MODEL`) as a 'Part'.

        See `Part.instances()` method for documentation.

        :return: pykechain.models.Part
        :raises: NotFoundError, MultipleFoundError
        """
        instances_list = list(self.instances())
        if len(instances_list) == 1:
            return instances_list[0]
        else:
            raise MultipleFoundError("Part {} has more than a single instance. "
                                     "Use the `Part.instances()` method".format(self.name))
```

It should look something like this:

```
    def instance(self):
        """
        Retrieve the single (expected) instance of this 'Part' (of `Category.MODEL`) as a 'Part'.

        See `Part.instances()` method for documentation.

        :return: pykechain.models.Part
        :raises: NotFoundError, MultipleFoundError
        """
        instances_list = list(self.instances())
        if len(instances_list) == 1:
            return instances_list[0]
        elif len(instances_list) > 1:
            raise MultipleFoundError("Part {} has more than a single instance. "
                                     "Use the `Part.instances()` method".format(self.name))
       elif len(instances_list) < 1:
            raise NotFoundError("Part {} has no instance. "
                                     "Use the `Part.instances()` method".format(self.name))
```

issue_body:
Hi, it seems that a unsatisfiable floating-point constraint is checked to be sat. The constraint is as followed:
 x1*x1+x2*x2+x3*x3+x4*x4 < 0
 and all the variables can not be infinite and NAN.

 The constraint is translated as the SMTLIB format as following file,  
[fp-smt220160623.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1097278/fp-smt220160623.txt)
with the last version Z3-4.5.1  and ubuntu12.04-64 bit,  I get a sat result.
Thanks.

issue_title: QF_ALIA not recognized
issue_body:
Hi,

When calling z3 with option `-smt2`
```
(set-logic QF_ALIA)
```
results in the following output:
```
WARNING: unknown logic, ignoring set-logic command
```

Although QF_ALIA is not explicitly listed in the “Logics” section in the smt-lib.org web site, it appears in the graph of logics in that same page. Also there is definitely a QF_ALIA category in the 2014-06-03 SMT-LIB benchmark repository.

Best regards,

-- David Déharbe.

issue_title: sorts inhabited by default?
issue_body:
Hi all,

on the VC below Z3 answers "unsat" although one could argue that it's not ... the critical question is whether sorts are always inhabited or not. Clearly Z3 assumes that yes, but the SMTlib standard doesn't say anything about this as far as I can see. And other SMT-solvers (I tried CVC4 and Alt-ergo) don't exploit this.

So what is your opinion on this? Thanks in advance,

Johannes

================ VC below ===================

(declare-sort t 0)

(define-fun in_range ((x Int)) Bool (and (<= 1 x) (<= x 0)))

(declare-fun to_rep (t) Int)

;; range_axiom
  (assert (forall ((x t)) (in_range (to_rep x))))

(check-sat)

issue_title: Double->Float conversion broken
issue_body:
This used to work until a very recent builds. I suspect the fix to http://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/331 might have introduced it.

This benchmark converts to smallest possible double denormal (5.0e-324) to a single-precision value:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)
(define-fun s2 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53) ((_ to_fp 11 53) roundNearestTiesToEven (/ 1 )))
(declare-fun s0 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))
(declare-fun s1 () (_ FloatingPoint  8 24))
(assert
   (let ((s3 (fp.eq s0 s2)))
   (let ((s5 ((_ to_fp 8 24) roundNearestTiesToEven s0)))
   (let ((s6 (fp.eq s1 s5)))
   (let ((s7 (and s3 s6)))
   s7)))))
(check-sat)
(eval s1)
```

The result should be `+0`, but Z3 says `+oo`:

```
sat
(_ +oo 8 24)
```

A similar issue exists when converting `-5.0e-324` as well.

issue_title: Bug with replace
issue_body:
This is similar to https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/1594, and perhaps it's the same root-cause.

For this benchmark:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic ALL)
(define-fun s () String (str.replace "hey" "" "here"))
(check-sat)
(get-value (s))
```

z3 is producing (both regular and str3 solvers):

```
$ z3 b.smt2
sat
((s "hey"))
$ z3 smt.string_solver=z3str3 b.smt2
sat
((s "hey"))
```

The correct answer should be "herehey", as found by CVC4:

```
$ cvc4 b.smt2
sat
((s "herehey"))
```

since the empty string "occurs" at the beginning always. This is also explicitly stipulated by the new string logic:

> ; Replace 
>   ; (str.replace s t t') is the string obtained by replacing the first occurrence 
>   ; of t in s, if any, by t'. Note that if t is empty, the result is to prepend
>   ; t' to s; also, if t does not occur in s then the result is s.
>   ; Total
>   (str.replace String String String String)

issue_title: Java API equals() method of class "FuncDecl"
issue_body:
I find that in the Java API, each time the method getFuncDecl() in class Expr is called, a new instance of FuncDecl will be created and returned. However, the equals() method in FuncDecl basically returns the result of "this==o". As a result, two FuncDecl instances will be "not equal", even if they refer to the same function declaration. I guess it would be more reasonable if the equals() method returns "(this.getId()==((FuncDecl)o).getId)". Moreover, the hashCode() method might also need to be modified.

The class Symbol has a similar problem.

issue_title: Bug in CouchDatabase and Document exists() False condition
issue_body:
This bug exists in versions 2.1.0 down to 2.0.0 alpha for any version of Python.

The problem is in the `exists()` methods for both [CouchDatabase](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/master/src/cloudant/database.py#L98-L105) and [Document](https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/master/src/cloudant/document.py#L95-L105) whereby `True` is returned if the response code is 200 and `False` if it is anything else which is not exactly valid.

In the case of a `False` exists condition, the logic should be changed to specifically check for a 404 response code and return `False` otherwise `raise_for_status()` should be called.  This way we accurately relay whether a db or document actually exists remotely and we also pass back other problems to alert the caller when necessary.

Also, it would probably be better to use the Python requests `head` method rather than `get` for these `exists()` methods since we are really not concerned with any body content in these calls.

issue_title: Provide a mechanism to get the Olson name of a zone
issue_body:
This may not always be possible, but I think it could be very useful, and the overwhelmingly most common case here is that `tzfile` objects are created by `tz.gettz(OLSON_NAME)`, where at *some* point we at least had this information. Worst case scenario we pass this directly to `tzfile` and expose it as something like `zone_name`.

It is also worth considering switching over `__str__` so that it returns the zone name. That breaks backwards compatibility, but I'm not sure how many people are relying on it in the first place...
