issue_title: Can -input be /dev/stdin?
Proposal for storing volumetric data
issue_body:
Posting here for feedback/discussion.

## Summary

It would be useful to be able to:

* Make it easy (via a mongo query) to see what volumetric data is available for a given task doc

* Optionally, store the volumetric data to make it easy to retrieve via a mongo query

The benefits of this are mainly:

* Easier aggregation and building (particularly for bader and other topological analysis, also for  processing of locpots)

* We're looking into charge density visualization in the future, and need to be able to easily retrieve chgcars to be able to do this

## Approaches

Two ideas:

1. In the Atomate Drone, in each `calcs_reversed`, store a list of available volumetric data files. In `insert_task`, we then optionally look up the files requested by the user and insert these into gridfs, e.g. `insert_task(store_volumetric=('chgcar', 'locpot'))`.

or

2. In each `calcs_reversed`, we store the full volumetric data file as a string when generating the taskdoc, e.g. `calcs_reversed[0]['volumetric_data']['chgcar'] = chgcar_string`. However, in `insert_task`, we choose whether to keep this string and store it via insertion into gridfs, or whether to just discard the string from the taskdoc and not store it at all.

Discussing with @shyamd, we were perhaps erring towards option 2, but I thought it'd be useful to open this to feedback from others. The problem with 2 is that it involves storing a lot of data in the taskdoc, even if temporarily, but this is basically what we do with bandstructure and dos objects so it seemed logically cleaner.

I have a draft implementation for option 1 available (https://github.com/hackingmaterials/atomate/compare/master...mkhorton:chgcar, changes to `VaspDrone` have been tested, changes to `insert_task` have not) to give an idea for what this might look like -- I think the changes to `VaspDrone` seem reasonable?

issue_title: Finalize the way that plugins are loaded/enabled/disabled during start
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

We have seen some load errors due to module load order. We should clarify how all this works and make a nice API (based on current conventions) to support developers and make sure that plugins have a long-term life span.

This issue is taking over from #186 where we fixed a bug due to this unclear  module load order.

 * [ ] Clearly describe what should happen in a plugin's enable and disable method callbacks
 * [ ] Decide whether to track plugin version changes in an installation?
 * [ ] Decide how and when they should be handled

### Expected behavior

re: my own post

> Following up on this: Since one may run `enable->disable->enable`, with each operation starting and exiting Kolibri, I believe that plugins should not make lots of assumptions about initial states while running `enable`. This is just a caution.

The above is about making assumptions such as "enable is an atomic action that will only be called once". This is easy to assume, however kolibri (the mother app) will not be able to give such guarantees - It maybe have been called before with a different plugin version. However, Kolibri's base plugin classes can provide the default behavior needed for common plugins. No plugin should ever have to run migrations and collectstatic.

> Anyways, because commands such as `collectstatic` and `migrate` have to be run, we also have to run `django.setup()` anyways.

Which is because while running any kind of setup command, we may detect changes in the version of kolibri or any of its plugins.

> Here's a rough vision of what enabling a plugin could look like:
> 
> ``` 
> $ kolibri plugin enable kolibri.plugins.learn
> 
> Enabling plugin: kolibri.plugins.learn
> 
> Verifying dependencies... OK!
> Collecting static assets (JS/CSS/images)........ OK!
> Running database migrations............ OK!
> 
> Plugin enabled. Please start/restart Kolibri.
> ```

### User-facing consequences

We are looking at some severe problems if plugin upgrades break, either in bundled core plugins or any other external plugin.

issue_title: Decimal objects should respect as_string argument at either the validation or deserialization stages
issue_body:
The `Decimal` object subclasses `Number` which establishes an `as_string` constructor parameter in order to serialize values as strings.

While this is a good idea and probably caters to a canonical use-case in serialization, there is the potential for unexpected behaviour in deserialization and/or validation (when using the `Decimal` object, specifically).

This is because the Python `decimal.Decimal()` method constructs values from integers, strings, floats, or tuples; and (per https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/decimal.html#quick-start-tutorial):

> Construction from an integer or a float performs an exact conversion of the value of that integer or float

From the Python documentation, even with `as_string` set, the current behaviour is to deserialize by passing directly to `decimal.Decimal()`, causing a situation like this:

```
>>> Decimal('3.14')
Decimal('3.14')
>>> Decimal(3.14)
Decimal('3.140000000000000124344978758017532527446746826171875')
```

This behaviour is at best arcane but at worst unexpected or wrong regardless of whether `as_string` is set.

I propose that when `as_string` _is_ set to true, either the `_deserialize` method implicitly casts the value to a string before passing to `decimal.Decimal()` or non-string values do not pass validation. Legacy behaviour would be preserved when `as_string` is false.

I prefer the implicit coercion to a string and can submit a small pull request to implement this. Failing validation is a bit more difficult because you have to differentiate between `str` and `unicode` - but I have presented it here as a potential solution to the problematic behaviour for comparison.

issue_title: idxstats in python3 returns a byte object instead of a string
issue_body:
I am using pysam version 0.9.0 and python3.4.

When running idxstats I get a bytes object instead of an string. Python 2 returns a string.

```
>>> idxstat = pysam.idxstats(bam_file)
>>> type(idxstat)
<class 'bytes'>
```

This is problematic because if one tries to split `idxstats` and error occurs:

```
>>> idxstat.split("\n")
TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'str'
```

The solution is to use `decode()` to convert the bytes object into a `str` but I think that pysam should return a string by default. 

This problem could be related to pysam opening a file as binary (see http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33054527/python-3-5-typeerror-a-bytes-like-object-is-required-not-str)

issue_title: "SystemError" Error returned without exception set on cigar strings with soft clipping without query sequence present
issue_body:
Hello !

I've noticed a possible issue with psyam with BAM file. I noticed for alignments that did not have query sequences stored (e.g., "*", according to the BAM standard, query_sequence is not required) that ```query_alignment_end``` (```qend```) and ```query_alignment_length``` (```qlen```) return an exception: 

```SystemError: error return without exception set```

It seems that soft clipping of 1 (e.g., 124=1S) with an alignment without a query sequence will cause this exception. Changing the cigar string to have soft clipping of 2 (e.g., 123=2S), results in -2 being returned for ```qend```.

In https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/master/pysam/calignedsegment.pyx:

The function definition specifies an exception is raised when -1 is returned. 

```cdef inline int32_t getQueryEnd(bam1_t *src) except -1:```

I think what is happening here is that since the query sequence is not present, the length = 0.
```cdef uint32_t end_offset = src.core.l_qseq```

Then later when end offset is modified for soft clipping:
```elif op == BAM_CSOFT_CLIP:
                end_offset -= cigar_p[k] >> BAM_CIGAR_SHIFT```

end_offset is being set to -1, returning the exception. The result of -2 for ```qend``` when soft clipping is 2, seems to confirm this.

The behavior I expected was for pysam to calculate the ```qend``` using the cigar string when the query sequence is not present. 


Here's a representative portion of the sam file that caused be to discover the issue.
```NODE_666_length_125_cov_612.438_ID_49376^I0^INC_001141_chr_09^I433573^I60^I1S4=1X6=1X10=1X5=1I44=1X50=^I*^I0^I0^IGGAAACGGGTTTCATAAGGCACCCCGGCACCCCTATAATTGGCATTCCCACATTCTGCGCATACGAATACACATAAGTGCCATAAAACAATACTCCACATACGAAGTCTATGAAAGATGTATGAT^I*^INM:i:5^IAS:i:108^IXB:f:200.558^IXE:Z:5.35e-52$

issue_title: Set application_name database options by default in shell_plus
issue_body:
On production (and other) system it may be important to identify SQL queries origin, there is a mecanism in PostgreSQL called **application_name** I suggests to automatically set this in *shell_plus*, it may be set two in other Database System. It's easy to set this by adding an **OPTIONS** in `settings.DATABASES`, I'll propose a PR to do this

With right log configuration you'll see lines like theses in your logs :

```
[4-1] user=rodo,db=foo,host=10.0.10.1,app=shell_plus LOG:  duration: 50.004 ms  statement: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM bar
[5-1] user=rodo,db=foo,host=10.0.10.1,app=worker1 LOG:  duration: 45.434 ms  statement: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM bar WHERE id = 42
[6-1] user=rodo,db=foo,host=10.0.10.2,app=worker2 LOG:  duration: 42.998 ms  statement: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM bar WHERE id = 37
```

The first two lines just defers by the **application_name** (parameter %a in `log_line_prefix` postgres config entry), as is you can  immediatly know that bad query without a where clause was done by a human using the *shell_plus* and it's not in your code.
 
Ref :  http://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/static/libpq-connect.html#LIBPQ-CONNECT-APPLICATION-NAME

issue_title: SparkMagic and Livy user impersonation
issue_body:
### Background ###

When a Spark notebook is executed in Jupyter, SparkMagic sends code (REST API) to Livy which then creates a Spark job and submits it to a YARN cluster for execution. By default YARN jobs thus submitted run as user **livy** but many enterprise organizations want Jupyter users to be impersonated in Livy. This can be achieved by enabling Livy impersonation and adding the ```proxyUser``` property in the spark magic configuration for each user that needs to be impersonated.

```
  "session_configs": {
    "driverMemory": "1000M",
    "executorCores": 2
    "proxyUser": "bob"
  },
```

The result is of this config change is that if **bob** is the Notebook instance user, they are now also the user running the YARN application.

```
Application-Id	    Application-Name	    Application-Type	      User	     Queue
application_1526925378944_0005	      livy-session-1	               SPARK	       bob	   default
```

### Proposal ###

Since the ```proxyUser``` value cannot be known a-priori, it must be set individually for every user in their spark magic config json. This is not ideal because it increases configuration complexity for a multi-user enterprise requiring them to inject this property when a new user(s) is added to the system.

I'm proposing that SparkMagic support user impersonation by default - meaning it always sends the ```proxyUser``` with its value as the user name of process SparkMagic is running in. This elimiates configuration complexity and makes spark magic more amenable for enterprise use. Users can always override proxyUser by explicitly setting its value in ```sessions_configs``` json object and that will take precedence over the default solution of using the OS user for impersonation.

I envision this to be low complexity change in /sparkmagic/utils/configuration.py. Happy to get some feedback on this proposal.

# allow large results

# do not flatten results

# set query priority; choices {"BATCH", "INTERACTIVE"}
job_config.priority = "INTERACTIVE"
# # dry run for testing
# if TEST is True:
#     job_config.dry_run = True
# else:
#     job_config.dry_run = False

# run the query
query_job = bq_client.query(BQ_QUERY,
                            job_config=job_config)

# batched jobs may not be run immediately but interactive should,
# but we still need to wait
while query_job.done() is False:
    print("waiting for query to finish.")
    time.sleep(3)
    continue
print("query finished.")


```

issue_title: Storage: support for Bucket.location setter
issue_body:
I was trying to change location of the bucket in Storage, but everything gets updated apart from location. It looks like the data is being sent through API, but are not persisted?

Code example
```python
storage_client = StorageClient()
bucket = storage_client.create_bucket(name)
bucket.storage_class = 'MULTI_REGIONAL'
bucket.location = 'EU'
bucket.labels = {
     'project': 'etl-transformer',
     'group': 'etl',
}
bucket.patch()
```

```python
# update_properties printed in _PropertyMixin:patch
{
    'labels': {
        'project': 'etl-transformer',
        'group': 'etl'
    },
    'storageClass': 'MULTI_REGIONAL',
    'location': 'EU'
}

# api_reponse
{
    u 'labels': {
      u 'project': u 'etl-transformer',
      u 'group': u 'etl'
    },
    u 'location': u 'US',
    u 'storageClass': u 'MULTI_REGIONAL'
}
```

I know that documentation doesn't say that `location` attribute is a setter, but under the hood it's a `_scalar_property` which is append to changed properties and according to json API, location is writable:

https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/buckets

issue_title: Use original file/directory path as input, not one mapped under tmpdir
issue_body:
Hi, I'm using cwltool to run a workflow which has a step to execute [STAR](https://github.com/alexdobin/STAR). STAR has a feature to load the reference genome index to the Linux Shared Memory to share data among different processes to reduce loading time (explained in the page 8 of https://github.com/alexdobin/STAR/blob/master/doc/STARmanual.pdf).

The problem is that this feature cannot be used via cwl-runner, since cwl-runner passes the input directory path mapped under tmpdir to STAR, while STAR identifies if the reference data is already loaded using the input directory path.

I understand this feature will not work with docker, but I'm wondering if there is any way to use this feature on non-docker execution. Is it possible to use the original directory path as an input, not using one mapped to the tmp?

issue_title: Race condition in cwltool parallel mode
issue_body:
I have been testing the parallel mode for cwltool using the latest code on master (addcaa2) so I could remove that logic from cwl-tes.  I found through running the conformance tests that there is a race condition in the MultithreadedJobExecutor.  

The race condition is between removing the thread from the threads set and the exception being appended to the exceptions array.  I found that some portion of the time that this loop https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/master/cwltool/executors.py#L170-L171 would break too early and miss an exception due to the problem described above.

issue_title: Exception when iDisplayLength=-1 (all)
issue_body:
In version '0.9.0', when you make a request to DatatableView with iDisplayLenght set to -1 (all registers), the following exception is raised:

UnboundLocalError at / local variable 'object_list' referenced before assignment
Traceback:
File "/home/javier/.virtualenvs/ssbb_dev/lib/python3.4/site-packages/datatableview/datatables.py" in _get_current_page
1.         return object_list

The code of _get_current_page method of Datatable class have an if statement that leaves the variable object_list unassigned when self.config['page_length'] == -1

You can fix it adding a line with object_list = self._records just before the if.

Best regards.

issue_title: webui prefix
issue_body:
i want to be able to set a prefix for the webui to get it run under a different path host by apache2_mod_uwsgi.

apache2.conf.sample
<VirtualHost *:80>
ProxyPass /test uwsgi://127.0.0.1:5002/
ProxyPassReverse  /test uwsgi://127.0.0.1:5002/
</VirtualHost>

the uwsgi should  redirect to 
http://testserver.prv/test/login
but its allways redirecting to 
http://testserver.prv/login
